title: Sodium-Ion Battery (SIB)
focus: Characteristics, advantages, and recent developments of Sodium-Ion Batteries compared to Lithium-Ion Batteries.
rationale: SIBs are a crucial alternative to LIBs. UPSC often asks comparative questions on battery technologies, focusing on energy density, safety, and charge carriers.
sectionExcerpt: A research team under the Department of Science and Technology developed a super-fast charging SIB that charges up to 80% in six minutes and lasts over 3000 cycles. SIBs use Na+ instead of Li+. They generally have lower energy density than Li-ion but offer better safety as they can be transported at zero voltage, reducing fire risks.
evidenceBullets: Developed by a DST team: charges to 80% in 6 minutes, lasts over 3000 charge cycles.
Uses sodium ions (Na+) to carry charge instead of lithium ions (Li+).
Has lower energy density compared to Lithium-ion batteries.
Higher safety profile: can be transported at zero voltage (fully discharged), which reduces fire risks.

title: Vanadium Redox Flow Battery and Flow Batteries
focus: Mechanism, applications, and types of flow batteries, specifically highlighting the VRFB inaugurated at NTPC NETRA.
rationale: Grid-scale energy storage is a major UPSC theme. The inauguration of India's first MWh-scale VRFB makes the mechanism and application of flow batteries highly relevant for Prelims.
sectionExcerpt: India's largest and first MWh-Scale Vanadium Redox Flow Battery (VRFB) was inaugurated at NTPC NETRA. Flow batteries are powered by redox reactions involving two different liquid electrolytes that pass ions through a porous membrane. They can store large amounts of energy for grid-scale applications and do not use flammable or polluting materials. Types include Vanadium Redox, Zinc-Bromine, and Iron-Salt/Organic systems.
evidenceBullets: India's first MWh-Scale VRFB was inaugurated at NTPC NETRA.
Powered by reduction-oxidation (redox) reactions using two different liquid electrolytes and a porous membrane.
Suitable for grid-scale applications, storing large amounts of energy without using flammable or polluting materials.
Types of flow batteries include Vanadium Redox, Zinc-Bromine, and Iron-Salt/Organic systems.

title: Solid-State Batteries (SSBs)
focus: Composition and advantages of Solid-State Batteries over conventional liquid electrolyte batteries.
rationale: SSBs represent a major technological leap in energy storage. Questions can focus on their solid electrolytes, safety improvements, and energy density compared to conventional batteries.
sectionExcerpt: Solid-State Batteries use solid electrolyte solutions, eliminating the need for a different separator. They offer improved safety as solid electrolytes are less prone to leakage or combustion. SSBs also have the potential to store more energy in the same volume or weight and can recharge 4 to 6 times faster than liquid electrolyte batteries.
evidenceBullets: Use solid electrolyte solutions, which do not require a different separator.
Offer improved safety because solid electrolytes are less prone to leakage or combustion compared to liquid electrolytes.
Have the potential to store more energy in the same volume or weight (higher energy density).
Capable of recharging up to 4 to 6 times faster than conventional liquid electrolyte batteries.
